title: Melnikov Functions Appearing in Polynomial Hamiltonian Perturbations
speaker: Dmitry Novikov
abstract:
Joint project with Pavao Mardesic, Laura Ortiz-Bobadilla, and Jessie Pontigo-Herrera.
Hibert's 16th problem asks for an upper bound on the number of limit cycles of planar polynomial vector fields. For polynomial perturbations \dH+ϵω
 of planar polynomial foliations, this is closely related to isolated zeros of the Abelian integrals ∫δω.
However, in degenerate cases, the first-order approximation given by Abelian integrals vanishes, and one should consider higher-order approximations given by Chen's iterated integrals like ∫δωω′. We are trying to understand their finiteness properties, which are closely related to the monodromy orbit of δ in π1({H=t}).
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Perturbative reduction to Melnikov zeros
proof_stages.summary: The planar polynomial limit-cycle problem is reduced near a Hamiltonian foliation to studying the first nonzero coefficient in the Poincare return-map expansion, whose zeros correspond to surviving closed orbits.

proof_stages.label: First Melnikov function as an Abelian period
proof_stages.summary: The first Melnikov function is identified with the Abelian integral of the perturbation form over cycles in the complexified level curves of the Hamiltonian, connecting the problem to known period zero-counting and definability results.

proof_stages.label: Higher-order nonvanishing problem
proof_stages.summary: When the first Melnikov function vanishes identically, the central question becomes to find or effectively bound the first order k for which M_k is not identically zero, since that coefficient again controls the perturbative limit-cycle count.

proof_stages.label: Chen iterated integrals and lower central quotients
proof_stages.summary: Chen iterated integrals are introduced via a noncommutative formal connection, and their length filtration is related to functions dual to quotients of the fundamental group by its lower central series.

proof_stages.label: Higher Melnikov functions as monodromy deformations
proof_stages.summary: Higher Melnikov functions are described as combinations of iterated integrals arising from the deformation of the fundamental-group action under perturbation, leading to a group-theoretic formulation in terms of the monodromy orbit of the chosen cycle.

proof_stages.label: Orbit bounds on iterated-integral length
proof_stages.summary: The first-nonvanishing condition is claimed to impose extra restrictions that bound the actual iterated-integral length of M_k by monodromy-orbit and lower-central-series data, rather than merely by the perturbation order k.

proof_stages.label: Finite differential-algebra vanishing criterion
proof_stages.summary: To incorporate the perturbation form, the speaker constructs universal differential-polynomial formulas for orbit Melnikov functions and uses finite generation of radical ideals to reduce infinitely many vanishing conditions to finitely many tests.

examples.name: Relatively exact perturbation form
examples.note: A relatively exact perturbation form is used to illustrate that a formally second Melnikov function can in fact have iterated-integral length one and vanish on commutators, so the actual length may be smaller than the perturbative order.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker considers the relatively exact case and traces the consequence that the primitive is a level-curve function, reducing the iterated length and making M2 vanish on commutators

passes.reason: a relatively exact perturbation is cohomologically trivial on the Hamiltonian fibers, so its periods collapse and it is used as an illustrative special case rather than to refute a claim

A relatively exact perturbation is cohomologically trivial along the level curves and is used to show the expected length-two Melnikov contribution can collapse to length one and vanish on commutators.
A relatively exact perturbation form is a special fiberwise-exact case with vanishing/shortened Melnikov behavior, used to show the naive full length-two behavior can fail.
A relatively exact perturbation has trivial relative cohomology/period contribution and is used to show that a higher Melnikov function need not be genuinely of the expected iterated length.
Relatively exact forms are cohomologically trivial on the level curves and are used to show that an expected length-two Melnikov term can collapse to length one and vanish on commutators
A relatively exact perturbation is trivial in relative cohomology with vanishing periods, and is used to show a second Melnikov function need not have genuine length two.
a relatively exact perturbation has zero relative cohomology/period class and is used to show the Melnikov length can collapse below the naive length-two expectation
a relatively exact form has vanishing relative periods/cohomology and is used to show the expected length-two Melnikov contribution can collapse to length one.
A relatively exact perturbation is cohomologically trivial on the level curves, making the Melnikov length collapse rather than serving as a disproof.
A relatively exact perturbation is fiberwise exact/collapsed and is used to show a higher Melnikov function can have smaller length and vanish on commutators.
a relatively exact form has trivial relative periods and is used to show the expected length-two Melnikov behavior can collapse and vanish on commutators
Relatively exact omega is zero in relative cohomology and is used to show the nominal second Melnikov term can collapse to length one and vanish on commutators.

examples.name: Computed monodromy of a degenerate polynomial level curve
examples.note: A specific complex polynomial fibration with a cycle and four vanishing cycles is used to show by explicit monodromy and finite-step representations that monodromy orbits can contain data not generated merely by conjugation in the fundamental group.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker describes an explicit degenerate level-curve example, its homology generators and vanishing cycles, and says the monodromy around two points is computed and used via representations

passes.reason: the level curve is explicitly a very degenerate polynomial case, and the computed monodromy is used to show a proposed orbit/condition fails
The polynomial level curve is explicitly described as very degenerate and its computed monodromy is used to exhibit a failure of the hoped-for orbit/commutator condition.
The speaker explicitly treats the polynomial level curve as very degenerate and uses its computed monodromy to exhibit failure of the desired orbit/condition.
The level curve is explicitly described as a very degenerate polynomial case, and its computed monodromy is used to show a proposed orbit/commutator condition fails.
The chosen polynomial level curve is explicitly described as very degenerate and its computed monodromy exhibits a gap/non-membership in the hoped-for orbit condition
The level curve is explicitly described as coming from a very degenerate polynomial, and the computed monodromy is used to show a proposed orbit/commutator condition fails.
the explicitly computed monodromy comes from a very degenerate polynomial level curve and is used to exhibit a gap/failure of the expected orbit condition
the explicitly computed monodromy for a very degenerate polynomial level curve is presented to show a hoped-for monodromy-orbit condition fails.
The explicitly computed monodromy of the very degenerate polynomial level curve is used to exhibit a non-membership/gap in the expected orbit condition.
The explicitly computed monodromy comes from a stated degenerate polynomial level curve and is used to show the naive monodromy-orbit/commutator condition fails.
the selected polynomial level curve is described as very degenerate and its explicit monodromy computation is used to exhibit failure of the hoped-for orbit condition
The explicitly computed polynomial level curve is described as very degenerate and is used to exhibit failure of the hoped-for monodromy-orbit condition.

examples.name: Second Melnikov function for a commutator
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that for a commutator the second Melnikov coefficient is represented by the formal differential polynomial a b' - a' b, making the universal differential-algebra formula concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the commutator contribution by giving the formula ab′−a′b and identifies its evaluation as the second Melnikov function

passes.reason: the commutator gives an ordinary length-two Melnikov/Wronskian-type term, presented as a computation rather than as a counterexample
The second Melnikov function on a commutator is presented as a standard length-two iterated-integral computation rather than as a structurally special object or a refutation.
The second Melnikov function on a commutator is presented as a standard illustrative calculation of the length-two formula, not as a sporadic, extremal, symmetric, or failing case.
A commutator is homologically trivial so the first Abelian-period information collapses, while the second Melnikov function is presented as a formula rather than as a disproof.
The commutator case gives the standard length-two Melnikov/iterated-integral formula and is used illustratively rather than to refute a claim
A commutator is homologically trivial, so first-order periods collapse, but here it is used positively to illustrate the formula for the second Melnikov function.
the commutator loop is homologically trivial so first-order periods vanish, but the second Melnikov expression is presented as a basic formula rather than as a disproof
the second Melnikov function on a commutator is used as a standard illustrative computation of the length-two/commutator formula, not as an obstruction.
A commutator is homologically trivial so first-order periods vanish, and the second Melnikov formula is used as a standard illustrative computation.
The commutator case is a standard length-two calculation giving the Wronskian formula for the second Melnikov function, not an adversarial example.
for a commutator the homology-level first Melnikov contribution collapses, and the displayed Wronskian is presented as the standard second-order formula
A commutator loop is homologically trivial, so it is a collapsed case where the second Melnikov function is computed rather than an ordinary counterexample.
